source_metadata.title_raw: Lan George - A-19-087 - June 5, 2019 - Arroyo Grande

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
· 
1102 Q Street• Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Lan George 
City Councilmember 
City of Arroyo Grande 
501 Castillo Court 
Arroyo Grande, CA 93420 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-19-087 
Dear Ms. George: 
June 5, 2019 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other conflict of interest laws. Also note that 
we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (in re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any 
advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts 
underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit you, an Arroyo Grande City Councilmember, from taking part in 
governmental decisions relating to a proposal by Monterey Bay Community Power to replace 
Pacific Gas & Electric ("PG&E") as the City's energy provider given that your husband is a PG&E 
employee? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to the Monterey Bay 
Community Power proposal because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a 
material financial effect on your source of income interest in PG&E. · 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
PG&E is currently the City of Arroyo Grande's energy provider. The Arroyo Grande City 
Council will soon consider a proposal by Monterey Bay Community Power to replace PG&E as the 
City's energy provider. Your husband is a PG&E employee. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


ANALYSIS 
File No. A-19-087 
Page No. 2 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit a public official from taking part in a 
governmental decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision would have a material 
financial effect on one or more of the official's financial interests distinguishable from its effect on 
the public generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An official's interests that may give rise to a 
disqualifying conflict of interest under the Act are identified in Section 87103. With respect to 
decisions relating to the City's potential replacement of PG&E as the City's energy provider, you 
have the following interests, based on the facts presented: 
• 
A source of income interest in PG&E if you aggregate $500 or more in income from your 
community property interest in your spouse's income from PG&E in the 12 months prior to the 
decisions at issue.2 (See Section 87103(c).) 
• 
An interest in your personal finances and those of immediate family members. 3 (See Section 
87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 18701 ( a) provides that a decision's financial effect on an official's financial 
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in 
the decision; an official's interest is "explicitly involved" if the interest is a named party in, or the 
subject of, the decision; and an interest is the "subject of a proceeding" if the decision involves the 
issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or 
contract with, the interest. Regulation 18701(b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to a 
decision's effect on an official's interest that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides 
that the decision's effect on such an interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." 
PG&E is a named party in, or the subject of, decisions relating to the potential replacement 
of PG&E as the City's energy provider because PG&E currently contracts with the City to be the 
City's energy provider. Therefore, those decisions' effects on PG&E are presumed reasonably 
foreseeable because PG&E is explicitly involved in those decisions. 
Regulation 18702.3 sets forth the Act's materiality standards applicable to a decision's 
effect on an official's interest in a source of income, and_ subdivision (a)(l) of that regulation 
provides that: 
(a) Income from the Sale of Goods and Services: For income received by 
the official or his or her spouse for goods and services provided in the ordinary 
course of business, including a salary, the financial effect is material if: 
(1) The source is a claimant, applicant, respondent, contracting party, or is 
otherwise named or identified as the subject of the proceeding. 
2 Section 82030 defines "income" for purposes of the Act, and subdivision (a) of that section provides that 
"income" includes an official's community property interest in the income of a spouse. 
3 Section 82029 defines "immediate family" as the spouse and dependent children. 


File No. A-19-087 
Page No. 3 
Because PG&E is the City's current energy provider, it is a "contracting party" with respect 
to the City Council's decisions relating to the Monterey Bay Community Power proposal to replace 
PG&E as the City's energy provider. Therefore, it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions relating 
to the potential replacement of PG&E as the City's energy provider would have a material financial 
effect on PG&E.4 Accordingly, the Act prohibits you from talcing part in those decisions. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please-contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:aja 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
WJ.fJk 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division 
4 We do not analyze whether it is reasonably foreseeable that decisions relating to the Monterey Bay 
Community Power proposal would have a disqualifying effect on your interest in your personal finances because we 
have already determined that those decisions would have a disqualifying effect on your source of income interest in 
PG&E.

sections.question:
Does the Act prohibit you, an Arroyo Grande City Councilmember, from taking part in 
governmental decisions relating to a proposal by Monterey Bay Community Power to replace 
Pacific Gas & Electric ("PG&E") as the City's energy provider given that your husband is a PG&E 
employee?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. The Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to the Monterey Bay 
Community Power proposal because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a 
material financial effect on your source of income interest in PG&E. ·

QUESTION: Does the Act prohibit you, an Arroyo Grande City Councilmember, from taking part in 
governmental decisions relating to a proposal by Monterey Bay Community Power to replace 
Pacific Gas & Electric ("PG&E") as the City's energy provider given that your husband is a PG&E 
employee?

CONCLUSION: Yes. The Act prohibits you from taking part in decisions relating to the Monterey Bay 
Community Power proposal because it is reasonably foreseeable that those decisions would have a 
material financial effect on your source of income interest in PG&E. ·
